event_name: UFC Fight Night: Muhammad vs. Bonfim

fights.fighter_a_bio: Belal Muhammad pressures with volume striking and clinch work, grinding opponents down over three rounds. His striking defense is his weakness—he eats clean shots trying to close distance, which leaves him vulnerable to precise counters.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Gabriel Bonfim is a heavy grappler who hunts takedowns early and controls with top pressure; he's a major favorite here with a 74% win probability according to our model. His submission game is dangerous, but he can get caught in scrambles if opponents explode out of his control.
fights.watch_for: Whether Belal can keep the fight standing and make Bonfim miss on the way in—if Bonfim gets him down, it's a mismatch on the mat.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Baraniewski is relatively unproven at this level with a 1500 Elo rating matching a blank slate. Without clear fight film, expect to learn his tendencies live—he's a coin flip on paper.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Elekana has a razor-thin edge in our model (50.3%) with a marginally higher 1502 Elo, suggesting slightly more proven fight experience. The model likes him, but this is essentially a pick-em matchup.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the center of the octagon in round one will likely dictate pace—both fighters are so evenly matched that early aggression and ring generalship become the deciding factor.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Imanol Rodriguez is a striker who leans on volume and forward pressure to overwhelm opponents, mixing hands and kicks to keep defenses honest. Our model gives him a slight edge at 51%, but his Elo rating shows he's barely ahead—this is a coin flip fight.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matt Schnell operates in the clinch and off the back foot, looking to counter and create angles rather than initiate from distance. He's ranked almost identically to Rodriguez on proven experience, making this a true toss-up.
fights.watch_for: Rodriguez's ability to cut the cage and land clean combinations in the first round—if Schnell survives the early pressure and gets comfortable in the clinch by round two, he'll have the timing to pick Rodriguez apart on the counter.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bruno Silva lands hard jabs and crosses from distance, then chains takedowns when opponents drop their hands. Our model gives him a 66% edge—he's proven at this level with a 1468 Elo rating and uses his reach to set up entries cleanly.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Edgar Chairez presses forward aggressively and hunts submissions off his back, but he's less tested than Silva (1486 Elo masks a thinner resume). He thrives when scrambles get messy, but Silva's range control could keep this from turning into a brawl.
fights.watch_for: Whether Chairez can close the distance and force Silva into scrambles before getting caught by a clean counter. Silva staying patient at range = his fight to lose.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Jeisla Chaves relies on pressure and forward movement to dictate exchanges, forcing opponents into uncomfortable positions where her timing matters most. Our model gives her a slight edge at 52.4%, suggesting her approach edges out Duben's game.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Yuneisy Duben's fight record is thin on publicly available detail, but she'll need to disrupt Chaves' rhythm and avoid getting backed into the fence where pressure fighters thrive. At 47.6% in our model's math, she's the slight underdog here.
fights.watch_for: Whether Duben can create distance and counter when Chaves pushes forward—if she gets trapped against the cage, Chaves' pressure tactics will likely control the round.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Allen hunts the clinch to drag opponents down and control them on the mat, where he's methodical with ground-and-pound and submissions. He's patient enough to grind out decisions but explosive enough to finish if you leave openings.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Shahbazyan relies on heavy hands and forward pressure to overwhelm opponents early, mixing striking combos with sudden wrestling when he senses an opening. He can implode if he eats clean counters or gets stuck in a grappling battle he didn't initiate.
fights.watch_for: Whether Allen can establish his clinch game before Shahbazyan's power shots force him to backpedal—if Shahbazyan dictates the range, he wins; if Allen locks him up, he controls the fight.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bryce Mitchell is a suffocating top-control grappler who uses his frame and positioning to grind opponents into submission—he's at his best when he can drag fights to the mat and work from north-south or side control. His weakness: if he can't get the takedown or gets caught standing, he lacks the striking arsenal to create openings.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Victor Henry is a heavy favorite with a higher Elo rating, suggesting he's proven against tougher competition and can neutralize Mitchell's grappling through either striking distance management or scramble defense. Our model gives him an 91.8% win probability, primarily because his ability to avoid the mat or escape bottom position makes Mitchell's signature style much harder to execute.
fights.watch_for: Whether Mitchell can secure a takedown in the first two minutes—if he does, he controls the pace; if he stays on his feet past round two, Henry's striking advantage and cardio will likely run him down.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Marcus McGhee is a slight favorite here with a 56.9% win probability according to our model, backed by a 1547.9 Elo rating that reflects more proven performances. His edge suggests he either lands cleaner strikes, controls the clinch better, or dictates pace—but the even chaos index means this fight could flip fast.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jakub Wiklacz sits at 43.1% win probability with a 1500.0 Elo, meaning he's proven but slightly behind McGhee in our model's assessment. Equal chaos suggests he has a real path to victory if he forces his A-game and exploits any opening.
fights.watch_for: Whether Wiklacz can create scrambles and stay active early—if he lets McGhee settle into a rhythm and control the clinch or distance, the model's pick becomes heavy favorite territory fast.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Leavitt hunts takedowns early and often, using wrestling to drag opponents into his world where he can control and grind. He's most dangerous when he gets on top, but struggles if you keep him at distance and make him chase.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Brito mixes striking exchanges with takedown threats—he'll throw hands to set up the shot, then switch to grappling if it opens up. Our model favors him heavily (67.6%) because he can hurt you standing or chain submissions on the mat.
fights.watch_for: If Leavitt shoots within the first two minutes and gets the takedown, he can control rounds; if Brito keeps the fight standing and lands clean shots early, Leavitt will be too hurt to explode into his usual wrestling.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ziam mixes wrestling entries with sharp striking combinations—he hunts takedowns early to tire opponents, then picks them apart on the feet once they're gassed. Our model favors him heavily (76%) because his Elo rating shows he's beaten tougher competition and his wrestling gives him multiple paths to win.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Nolan is a pure striker who relies on footwork and timing to land clean shots without eating return fire. He's the underdog here because Ziam's wrestling poses problems Nolan hasn't solved at this level—our model gives him just a 24% chance.
fights.watch_for: Whether Nolan can keep the fight standing and land early before Ziam establishes his wrestling. If Ziam gets even one successful takedown in the first round, Nolan's cardio advantage disappears and the fight trends heavily in Ziam's favor.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ketlen Souza is a big favorite here (73% win probability per our model) and has the record to back it up—her Elo sits at 1485.8, meaning she's proven in close spots. She controls fights with pressure wrestling and ground-and-pound from top position, forcing opponents into survival mode.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ariane Carnelossi is the underdog despite a higher Elo rating (1515.1), likely because she hasn't faced comp at Souza's level recently. She'll look to stay on the outside with kicks and straight shots, but she struggles when pinned against the fence.
fights.watch_for: If Souza closes the distance and lands a takedown in the first round, she'll likely ride Carnelossi on her back for two rounds—but if Carnelossi keeps range with leg kicks and avoids clinch work, she can frustrate Souza into a decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Cachoeira pressures opponents with heavy hands and aggressive clinch work, forcing scrambles where her grappling dominates. She's a massive favorite here—the model gives her 94.3% to win—because Chandler hasn't shown the footwork or timing to keep her at distance.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Chandler relies on precision striking and lateral movement to stay away from power strikers, but she's undersized and tends to fold under sustained pressure. At just 5.7% win probability, she needs a perfect technical performance that her record doesn't suggest she can deliver.
fights.watch_for: Whether Chandler can use her footwork to escape Cachoeira's clinch attempts in the first two minutes—if she gets dragged inside, Cachoeira's wrestling advantage becomes impossible to overcome.
